title: Competition vs. Sectoral Regulators
content:
Session 10: Interface between competition and other regulatory enactments and  emerging issues 
Independent Sugar Corporation v. Girish Juneja [2025 | SC | ] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The present appeals arose out of CIRP initiated against HNGIL, a corporate debtor holding nearly 60% market share in the 
glass packaging industry in India ‚Äì SRA was AGI Greenpac, the second largest player in the same sector.  
‚û¢‚Äã The proposed resolution plan envisaged a combination between AGI Greenpac and HNGIL, which would result in 
an estimated combined market share of 80‚Äì85% in the FnB segment and 45‚Äì50% in the alcoholic beverage 
segment, thereby raising serious competition law concerns. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The appellant, Independent Sugar Corporation, had also submitted a resolution plan, which was rejected by the CoC.  
‚û¢‚Äã Although not aggrieved by the rejection of its own plan, the appellant challenged the approval granted to AGI 
Greenpac‚Äôs plan on the ground that it violated the mandatory requirement of prior approval from the CCI under 
the proviso to Section 31(4) of the IBC. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The EOI originally required resolution applicants to obtain CCI approval prior to CoC approval. 
‚û¢‚Äã However, this requirement was relaxed through an email issued by the RP, permitting applicants to obtain CCI 
approval after CoC approval but before filing the plan before the NCLT. 
‚ùñ‚Äã AGI Greenpac initially filed a Form I notification before the CCI, which was declared invalid. 
‚û¢‚Äã Subsequently, no application for approval was pending before the CCI when the CoC approved AGI Greenpac‚Äôs 
resolution plan.  
160 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Only after CoC approval did AGI Greenpac file Form II, along with a voluntary divestment proposal to address 
competition concerns.  
‚û¢‚Äã Meanwhile, the RP moved the NCLT under Section 30(6) of the IBC for approval of the plan. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The CCI ultimately approved the combination, but without issuing a show cause notice to HNGIL (the target entity) under 
Section 29(1).  
‚û¢‚Äã The NCLT rejected the appellant‚Äôs challenge, and the NCLAT upheld the plan, holding that while CCI approval 
was mandatory, prior approval before CoC consideration was only directory, as insisting on it could disrupt CIRP 
timelines. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the proviso to Section 31(4) of the IBC mandates that CCI approval of a proposed combination must be obtained 
prior to approval of the resolution plan by the CoC. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether an unsuccessful resolution applicant has a locus standi to challenge approval of a resolution plan on competition 
law grounds. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the CCI‚Äôs approval was vitiated due to non-compliance with mandatory procedural requirements under Section 29 
of the Competition Act, particularly the failure to issue notice to the target entity. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether there exists any irreconcilable conflict between the timelines under the IBC and the Competition Act. 
 
Rule: 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Standing of the Appellant 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court adopted a broad understanding of locus standi under both the IBC and the Competition Act.  
‚û¢‚Äã Once CIRP is initiated, proceedings become in rem, and not confined to private disputes. 
‚û¢‚Äã Under Section 53B of the Competition Act, any ‚Äúperson aggrieved‚Äù may maintain an appeal, and this term must be 
interpreted expansively.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
An unsuccessful resolution applicant whose plan could otherwise have been approved satisfies this 
requirement, particularly when the challenge relates to statutory non-compliance affecting the integrity of 
the CIRP. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Interpretation of the Proviso to Section 31(4) IBC 
‚û¢‚Äã The Supreme Court rejected the NCLAT‚Äôs view that prior CCI approval is merely directory. 
‚û¢‚Äã Applying the plain meaning rule, the Court held that the language of the proviso is clear and unambiguous, 
especially due to the use of the word ‚Äúprior‚Äù.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Where statutory language is explicit, courts cannot dilute its meaning on grounds of convenience or 
efficiency. 
‚û¢‚Äã The 2018 amendment was consciously introduced to ensure that competition law scrutiny precedes the exercise of 
the CoC‚Äôs commercial wisdom.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Any interpretation permitting post-approval scrutiny would defeat the legislative intent and render the 
proviso otiose. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Role of CCI vis-√Ý-vis the CoC‚Äôs Commercial Wisdom 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI has the statutory power to approve, reject, or modify a proposed combination.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Since the CoC‚Äôs commercial wisdom is paramount, it must be exercised only after the competition 
authority has completed its scrutiny.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Otherwise, the CoC may approve a plan that is legally unenforceable, or one that is later materially altered 
by CCI-mandated modifications, thereby undermining the decision-making process. 
‚û¢‚Äã A resolution plan containing a combination proposal that has not received prior CCI approval is therefore 
incapable of implementation and cannot acquire legal sanctity merely through CoC approval. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Illegality of Relaxation Granted by the Resolution Professional 
161 
 
‚û¢‚Äã The Resolution Professional performs a purely administrative function and has no authority to relax statutory 
requirements.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The email permitting post-CoC approval CCI clearance was ultra vires the IBC.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Once a plan is approved by the CoC, it cannot be modified, and therefore allowing competition scrutiny 
at a later stage creates an impermissible legal vacuum. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Procedural Violations under the Competition Act 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI failed to comply with Section 29(1) by not issuing a show cause notice to HNGIL, the target entity.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The definition of ‚Äúparties to the combination‚Äù under Regulation 2(f) expressly includes the target, even 
in insolvency situations.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Procedural safeguards under Sections 29(2)‚Äì29(6) are mandatory and cannot be bypassed merely because 
the Resolution Professional did not object. 
‚û¢‚Äã The use of the term ‚Äúinvestigation‚Äù under Section 29, as distinct from ‚Äúinquiry‚Äù under Section 26, was emphasised 
to highlight the depth and seriousness of merger scrutiny, which necessitates strict procedural compliance. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Voluntary Modifications and IBC Constraints 
‚û¢‚Äã The voluntary divestment proposed by AGI Greenpac under Regulation 25(1A) lacked the imprimatur of both 
parties to the combination.  
‚û¢‚Äã Further, divestment of assets of a corporate debtor necessarily attracts the provisions of the IBC, reinforcing the 
need for CCI scrutiny before CoC approval. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Timelines under IBC and Competition Act 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI approvals are ordinarily granted within 120 days, well within the outer limit of 330 days for CIRP.  
‚û¢‚Äã Relying on Essar Steel, the Court reiterated that timelines under the IBC are flexible in exceptional cases where 
delays are not attributable to the parties. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã SC affirmed the mandatory nature of the proviso to Section 31(4) of the IBC, holding that prior approval of the CCI is a 
sine qua non for any resolution plan containing a combination proposal.  
‚û¢‚Äã The AGI Greenpac resolution plan was held to be legally unsustainable, as it lacked prior CCI approval and 
suffered from serious procedural infirmities under the Competition Act. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Accordingly, the approval granted by the CoC and upheld by the NCLAT was set aside.  
‚û¢‚Äã All actions taken pursuant to the impugned resolution plan were nullified, and status quo ante was restored.  
‚û¢‚Äã The CoC was directed to reconsider the appellant‚Äôs resolution plan and any other plans that had obtained the 
requisite prior approval from the CCI. 
 
Kshitiz Arya v. Google [2025 | CCI] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The information was filed by Kshitiz Arya against Google, along with smart TV OEMs Xiaomi and TCL, alleging 
contravention of Ss 3 and 4.  
‚û¢‚Äã The core allegation concerned Google‚Äôs conduct in the Android Smart TV ecosystem, particularly through 
agreements entered into with smart TV OEMs, namely the Television App Distribution Agreement (TADA) and 
Android Compatibility Commitments (ACC). 
‚û¢‚Äã Google licenses the Android TV operating system to smart TV manufacturers.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Almost all Android smart TVs in India come pre-installed with Google‚Äôs proprietary applications, 
including the Play Store and YouTube. End-users are unable to change the pre-installed operating system, 
making access to Android TV a critical input for OEMs. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
It was alleged that Google leveraged this position to impose restrictive covenants on OEMs, mandating 
the pre-installation of the entire Google TV Services (GTVS) suite, prohibiting OEMs from using or 
developing forked versions of Android, and tying the YouTube app with the Play Store. 
‚ùñ‚Äã DG defined two relevant markets: 
‚û¢‚Äã the market for licensable smart TV device operating systems in India, and 
162 
 
‚û¢‚Äã the market for app stores for Android smart TV OS in India. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Google was found to be dominant in both markets and Google‚Äôs conduct amounted to abuse of dominance under multiple 
provisions of Section 4, including imposition of unfair conditions, denial of market access, restriction of technical 
development, and tying. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Subsequently, Google filed a Settlement Application under Section 48A of the Competition Act, read with the Competition 
Commission of India (Settlement) Regulations, 2024, seeking closure of proceedings based on proposed remedies and 
payment of a settlement amount.  
‚û¢‚Äã Google also sought a stay of proceedings pending consideration of the settlement. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Google‚Äôs conduct in mandating pre-installation of GTVS and restricting Android forks amounted to abuse of 
dominant position under Section 4 of the Act. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the tying of YouTube with the Play Store on Android TV violated Section 4(2)(e). 
‚ùñ‚Äã What is the legal effect of a settlement under Section 48A, and whether the settlement proposed by Google adequately 
addresses the competition concerns identified by the DG. 
‚ùñ‚Äã How the settlement amount is to be determined under the amended Section 27(b), the Settlement Regulations, 2024, and 
the Turnover Regulations, 2024. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 48A, introduced by the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, allows an enterprise to seek settlement of 
proceedings for alleged contraventions of Sections 3(4) and 4, subject to satisfaction of the Commission that the settlement 
addresses competition concerns.  
‚û¢‚Äã Acceptance of settlement results in closure of proceedings without a finding of contravention, and the order does 
not operate as precedent. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Under the Settlement Regulations, 2024, the Commission determines a base amount derived from the penalty framework 
under Section 27(b), and thereafter applies a 15% settlement discount. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Abuse through Mandatory Pre-installation under TADA 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission accepted the DG‚Äôs findings that mandatory pre-installation of the entire GTVS suite under 
TADA constituted an unfair condition imposed on smart TV OEMs.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Given Google‚Äôs dominance and the OEMs‚Äô lack of countervailing bargaining power, the requirement 
significantly curtailed OEM autonomy, reduced incentives to innovate, and foreclosed alternative app 
ecosystems.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
This conduct squarely attracted Sections 4(2)(a)(i) and 4(2)(d), as OEMs were compelled to accept 
obligations unrelated to the licensing of the Android TV OS. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Restrictions on Android Forks and Denial of Market Access 
‚û¢‚Äã Through Anti-Fragmentation Agreements (AFA) and ACC, Google prohibited OEMs from manufacturing or 
distributing devices based on forked Android operating systems, including non-GTVS versions.  
‚û¢‚Äã Such restrictions limited technical development and denied market access to developers of alternative Android 
forks, in violation of Sections 4(2)(b)(ii) and 4(2)(c).  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
These obligations were held to be supplementary and unrelated to the core licensing arrangement, further 
attracting Section 4(2)(d). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Tying of YouTube with the Play Store 
‚û¢‚Äã Google had tied the YouTube application with access to the Play Store on Android TV.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Given YouTube‚Äôs indispensability for smart TV users and the Play Store‚Äôs gatekeeping role, such tying 
strengthened Google‚Äôs ecosystem dominance and foreclosed competition, amounting to a violation of 
Section 4(2)(e). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Settlement under Section 48A ‚Äì Legal Effect 
163 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Acceptance of a settlement under Section 48A does not involve an adjudicatory finding of guilt. 
‚û¢‚Äã Instead, the Commission evaluates whether the settlement proposal sufficiently addresses the nature, gravity, and 
impact of the alleged contraventions, as identified by the DG.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
In the present case, the majority found that Google‚Äôs commitments adequately mitigated the competition 
concerns, justifying closure of proceedings. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Determination of Settlement Amount and Relevant Turnover 
‚û¢‚Äã Applying the amended Section 27(b), the Commission first identified Google‚Äôs relevant turnover.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Google argued that only revenue from Android TV OS licensing should be considered and that adopting 
a network effects approach, as done in the Android Smartphones case, would violate the Supreme 
Court‚Äôs ruling in Excel Crop Care, which mandates proportionality between the contravention and the 
penalty base. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
REJECTED ‚Üí revenue from YouTube on Android TV and the Play Store on Android TV is intrinsically 
linked to the relevant market.  
‚óè‚Äã
Since the abuse involved tying and leveraging across these services, their revenues formed an 
integral part of the competitive harm and could not be excluded. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission determined a base amount as a percentage of the average relevant turnover, adjusted for 
mitigating and aggravating factors.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Applying the mandatory 15% settlement discount, the final settlement amount was fixed at ‚Çπ20.24 crore. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission also rejected Google‚Äôs argument against retrospective application, noting that the settlement 
mechanism operates on current law and does not impose a punitive penalty but a consensual settlement amount. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Dissenting Opinion 
‚û¢‚Äã The proposed commitments did not sufficiently remedy the competition concerns identified by the DG, 
particularly the structural nature of Google‚Äôs control over the Android TV ecosystem. 
‚û¢‚Äã Allowing settlement in such circumstances risked under-enforcement in digital markets characterised by 
entrenched dominance and network effects. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã CCI (by majority) accepted Google‚Äôs Settlement Application under Section 48A, holding that the proposed remedies 
adequately addressed the identified abuses of dominance in the Android smart TV ecosystem.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Proceedings were closed without a finding of contravention, upon payment of a settlement amount of ‚Çπ20.24 crore. 
 
Bharti Airtel Limited v. Competition Commission of India [2018 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts:  
‚ùñ‚Äã RJIL filed info alleging that Bharti Airtel, Vodafone, and Idea (the incumbent dominant operators, or IDOs) had entered 
into an anti-competitive agreement/cartel in violation of 3(3).  
‚û¢‚Äã The allegation was that the IDOs collectively denied and delayed Points of Interconnection (POIs) to RJIL, thereby 
hindering its entry into the telecom market. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The telecom operators objected to CCI‚Äôs jurisdiction, contending that the telecom sector is comprehensively regulated by 
the Telegraph Act, 1885 and the TRAI Act, 1999.  
‚û¢‚Äã According to them, disputes relating to POIs, interconnection, licence conditions, and technical compatibility fall 
squarely within the exclusive domain of TRAI and TDSAT. 
‚ùñ‚Äã BHC held that while the Competition Act and TRAI Act are independent statutes, issues relating to telecom contracts, 
licences, and technical obligations must first be decided by the authorities under the TRAI Act. The matter reached the 
Supreme Court. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the CCI has jurisdiction to deal with alleged anti-competitive conduct in a sector regulated by the TRAI Act. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether TRAI has exclusive jurisdiction, thereby excluding the CCI altogether in telecom-related disputes. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the CCI can proceed simultaneously, or must await TRAI‚Äôs determination on sector-specific issues. 
164 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether writ petitions challenging a Section 26(1) order of the CCI are maintainable. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Nature of the Dispute and Sectoral Expertise 
‚û¢‚Äã The telecom sector is governed by a specialised statutory regime under the TRAI Act, designed to protect both 
service providers and consumers, regulate technical standards, and ensure orderly development of the sector.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The dispute raised by RJIL was not a pure competition law issue in isolation; rather, it directly involved 
compliance with licence conditions, interconnection obligations, and technical compatibility ‚Üí matters 
falling squarely within TRAI‚Äôs expertise. 
‚û¢‚Äã On facts, RJIL‚Äôs grievance that POIs were denied was essentially an allegation of non-compliance with licence and 
regulatory obligations.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Significantly, RJIL had already approached TRAI for redressal of these very issues. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Determining whether the IDOs were actually obliged to provide POIs, and whether there was technical 
feasibility or regulatory breach, required sector-specific adjudication that CCI was not institutionally 
equipped to undertake at the first instance. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Sequencing of Jurisdiction: TRAI First, CCI Later 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court rejected the argument that CCI‚Äôs jurisdiction is completely ousted in the telecom sector ‚Üí TRAI and 
CCI operate in distinct but complementary domains.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
While TRAI regulates the telecom sector and enforces licence obligations, CCI‚Äôs mandate is to assess 
competition law violations, such as cartelisation and abuse of dominance. 
‚û¢‚Äã However, the Court introduced an important sequencing principle: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
TRAI must first determine whether there has been a breach of telecom regulations, licence conditions, or 
technical obligations. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Only after TRAI‚Äôs findings can the CCI examine whether such conduct, if established, amounts to an 
anti-competitive agreement or abuse of dominance under the Competition Act. 
‚û¢‚Äã This approach avoids conflicting decisions, ensures regulatory coherence, and respects the specialised competence 
of sectoral regulators. 
‚ùñ‚Äã No Exclusive Jurisdiction of TRAI; No Implied Repeal of Competition Act 
‚û¢‚Äã The TRAI Act does not implicitly repeal the Competition Act, which addresses three specific types of conduct 
[anti-competitive agreements, abuse of dominance, and combinations] ‚Üí none of which fall within TRAI‚Äôs 
remedial framework in the same manner. 
‚û¢‚Äã Even if TRAI concludes that conduct is anti-competitive, its powers are limited to remedies under the TRAI Act.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Only the CCI can impose penalties, cease-and-desist orders, or structural remedies under Section 27 ‚Üí 
retains exclusive jurisdiction over competition law consequences, though it must defer to TRAI on 
sector-specific determinations. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Difference in Remedies and Institutional Design 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI‚Äôs remedies are qualitatively different.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Unlike TRAI, which primarily enforces compliance, CCI can impose structural remedies designed to 
reshape market structure, induce entry, and promote long-term competition.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Reinforces the need for CCI‚Äôs continued jurisdiction even in regulated sectors. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Maintainability of Writ Petitions against Section 26(1) Orders 
‚û¢‚Äã Relying on SAIL v. CCI, the Court reiterated that an order under Section 26(1) is administrative, not 
quasi-judicial, and ordinarily not amenable to writ jurisdiction.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
However, an exception exists where the challenge goes to the very jurisdiction of the authority. 
‚û¢‚Äã In the present case, since the telecom operators questioned CCI‚Äôs competence to even initiate proceedings, the writ 
petitions before the High Court were held to be maintainable under Article 226 ‚Üí tribunal cannot assume 
jurisdiction unless the jurisdictional facts exist. 
 
Conclusion: 
165 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã TRAI, as a specialised sectoral regulator, must first decide issues relating to telecom licences, interconnection obligations, 
and technical feasibility. 
‚ùñ‚Äã CCI‚Äôs jurisdiction is not excluded, but it must await TRAI‚Äôs findings before proceeding with competition law analysis. 
‚ùñ‚Äã There is no implied repeal of the Competition Act by the TRAI Act; both statutes must operate harmoniously. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Writ petitions challenging Section 26(1) orders are maintainable where the jurisdiction of the CCI itself is in question. 
 
166

cases:
Interface between competition and other regulatory enactments and  emerging issues 
Independent Sugar Corporation v. Girish Juneja
Bharti Airtel Limited v. Competition Commission of India
key_principles: ‚Äã The present appeals arose out of CIRP initiated against HNGIL, a corporate debtor holding nearly 60% market share in the
‚Äã The proposed resolution plan envisaged a combination between AGI Greenpac and HNGIL, which would result in
‚Äã The appellant, Independent Sugar Corporation, had also submitted a resolution plan, which was rejected by the CoC.
‚Äã Although not aggrieved by the rejection of its own plan, the appellant challenged the approval granted to AGI
